title: Proverbs 19
subtitle: Integrity Over Wealth; Patience; Kind to the Poor Lends to God

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — Integrity Over Wealth; Wisdom Yields Patience; Prudent Wife from the LORD

heb.paragraph: V.14: the prudent wife (ʾēšet maśkêlet — wife of understanding) is from the LORD (mēYHWH). Houses and wealth can be inherited through ordinary human means. But the prudent wife is a divine gift. The wise wife is not simply found; she is given.

V.11: wisdom (śekel) yields patience (ʾereḵ ʾappô — long of nose, slow to anger). The wise person's patience is not temperamental but theologically grounded: they understand enough to overlook offenses. Patience is the fruit of understanding.

panels.ctx: The first half of ch.19 addresses integrity over wealth (v.1), the isolation of poverty (vv.4,6-7), the false witness (vv.5,9), and wisdom-producing patience (v.11). The prudent wife (v.14) is the section's theological climax: she is a divine gift, not an inherited asset.

cross.note: He who finds a wife finds what is good and receives favour from the LORD — the direct parallel to v.14's prudent wife as a divine gift.
Blessed is the one who finds wisdom — the structure of "finding" that links wisdom, wife, and divine favour throughout Proverbs.

notes.note: A person's own folly leads to their ruin, yet their heart rages against the LORD. Self-caused disaster produces misdirected blame. The fool's path leads to ruin; when the ruin comes, they blame God.

A person's wisdom yields patience; it is to one's glory to overlook an offense. The long nose (slow-to-anger) is the product of understanding. Overlooking is often the wiser and more courageous response than retaliating. It is called glory because it requires strength, not weakness.

Houses and wealth are inherited from parents, but a prudent wife is from the LORD. The contrast establishes a hierarchy: property through ordinary human means; a wise spouse through divine appointment.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Many curry favour with a ruler — Alter notes this sardonic observation is a dry, realistic account of how social relationships work around power and wealth. Wisdom requires seeing social reality clearly, including its unflattering dimensions.

The false witness and liar who perish — the repetition of the false-witness consequence (cf. 19:5) forms a doublet that emphasises the point. Alter notes doublets in the proverb collection are a deliberate emphasis device.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The poor-with-integrity vs. rich-with-perverse-lips proverb is Proverbs' most direct challenge to equating wealth with virtue. Waltke notes the economic and moral hierarchies are distinct and can be inverted: the repeated 'better than' proverbs on integrity and poverty form a sustained argument.

A person's wisdom yields patience; it is glorious to overlook an offense. Waltke notes tiferet (splendour/glory) for the one who can overlook — the dignity of a person secure enough not to demand restitution for every slight. Patience is not weakness but the nobility of one who does not need to prosecute every wrong.

Calvin: 'When a man's folly ruins his life, his heart rages against the LORD — this is the nature of human self-deception. The person whose choices produced their misery blames God rather than themselves.'
Calvin on patience: 'The slow to anger who passes over an offense is the greater man. It is not weakness but magnanimity — the confidence of one who does not need every wrong acknowledged.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note traces the 'better than' poverty-integrity proverbs through 19:1, 19:22, and 28:6, identifying them as a sustained curriculum challenging economic-virtue conflation.
The NET note on tiferet (splendour/glory) for overlooking explains that the one who can overlook has an aesthetic quality — a beauty — in their freedom from the need for restitution.

sections.header: Verses 15–29 — Kind to the Poor Lends to the LORD; LORD's Purpose Prevails; Fear Leads to Life

heb.paragraph: V.17: the one who is kind to the poor (ḥônēn-dāl) is a lender to the LORD (malweh YHWH). The transaction is commercial in form but theological in content: kindness to the poor creates a debt that the LORD himself will repay. You are not giving to the poor; you are lending to God.
V.21: the LORD's purpose (ʿǎṣâ) is what prevails (tāqûm — stands, is established). The same sovereignty theme as 16:9. Human planning is real and multiplied; divine purpose is singular and final.

panels.ctx: The second half contains three of the chapter's most theologically significant verses: kindness to the poor as lending to the LORD (v.17), the LORD's purpose prevailing (v.21), and the fear of the LORD leading to life and contentment (v.23). These form the theological backbone: social ethics, sovereignty, and the life that flows from reverence.

cross.note: Whatever you did for the least of these you did for me — the NT fulfilment of v.17's lending-to-the-LORD principle.
The LORD establishes steps — the same divine sovereignty principle as v.21's prevailing purpose.

notes.note: Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD. The boldest statement of giving theology in Proverbs. The gift to the poor is framed not as charity but as a loan to God. The LORD repays. You are not losing; you are investing in the most reliable lender available.
Many are the plans in a person's heart, but it is the LORD's purpose that prevails. The companion to 16:9. Human planning is not futile but finite. The plans multiply; the purpose is one.
The fear of the LORD leads to life; then one rests content. The satisfaction of the person whose ultimate reference point is the LORD rather than circumstances.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The loan-to-the-LORD metaphor — Alter notes the audacity of the image is the point. Giving to the poor is presented with the reliability of a loan to the most creditworthy possible borrower. The commercial frame makes the theological claim vivid.
The hear-counsel / many-plans cluster — Alter notes human receptivity (v.20) paired with divine sovereignty (v.21) makes them complementary, not competing. Listen for wisdom; trust God with outcomes.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD — Waltke notes the commercial metaphor is startling: giving to the poor is a covenant transaction with God as borrower. The return is guaranteed by the most creditworthy possible debtor. This is one of Proverbs' most theologically radical statements.

Many plans in a person's heart, but the LORD's purpose prevails — Waltke notes this is the chapter's divine-sovereignty statement, parallel to 16:9. Human planning is real and proper; divine governance is comprehensive and prevailing.
Calvin: 'When we relieve the poor, we lend to God. God has identified himself with the poor as the recipient of generosity. The return is guaranteed by the most reliable possible guarantor.'

Calvin: 'Man proposes; God disposes. The many schemes of the human heart are not forbidden — planning is wisdom. But all human planning is enclosed within the divine purpose which ultimately prevails.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note cites Matt 25:40 as the NT counterpart: whatever you did for the least of these you did for me. The Proverbs principle reaches its fullest expression in the incarnation.
The NET note on etsat YHWH (the LORD's counsel/purpose) identifies this as one of the sovereignty proverbs parallel to 16:1–9, framing human planning within divine governance.

ppl.name: The kind-to-poor person
ppl.text: Social ethics reframed as a divine transaction.

ppl.name: The human planner
ppl.text: The wise person aligns plans with the LORD's purpose.

ppl.name: The fool who blames God
ppl.role: Rages against the LORD for self-caused ruin
ppl.text: The characteristic posture of the unexamined life.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD, and he will reward them for what they have done."NoteThe boldest statement of giving theology in Proverbs. Kindness to the poor is not charity but a loan to God himself, who repays. Matt 25:40 is its NT fulfilment.

src.title: Lending language
src.quote: Malweh (lender) is commercial vocabulary. The proverb deliberately uses financial language to frame charitable giving as a transaction with God.
src.note: The commercial metaphor reframes giving as investment, not loss.

src.title: LORD's purpose prevails (v.21)
src.quote: The same principle as 16:9 and 21:30. Repeated to emphasise the absolute finality of divine purpose over human planning.
src.note: The repetition is a deliberate emphasis of a foundational principle.

rec.text: Lending to the LORD — the primary OT text for giving as investment in divine purposes.
The LORD's purpose prevails — foundational to Reformed theology's emphasis on divine providence.

rows.label: The Poor and the LORD
rows.text: Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD. The lending-to-God metaphor is striking: generosity to the poor is a transaction with the God who repays. The poor person's need is the LORD's own request.

rows.label: The Poor and the LORD
rows.text: Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the LORD. The lending-to-God metaphor is striking: generosity to the poor is a transaction with the God who repays. The poor person's need is the LORD's own request.

rows.label: Integrity Over Wealth: The Economic-Moral Hierarchy — 19:1
rows.text: The repeated 'better poor with integrity than rich with perverse lips' systematically challenges the equation of wealth with virtue. In the wisdom tradition, economic and moral hierarchies are distinct and can be inverted.

hebtext.note: V.14: the prudent wife (ʾēšet maśkêlet — wife of understanding) is from the LORD (mēYHWH). Houses and wealth can be inherited through ordinary human means. But the prudent wife is a divine gift. The wise wife is not simply found; she is given.

V.11: wisdom (śekel) yields patience (ʾereḵ ʾappô — long of nose, slow to anger). The wise person's patience is not temperamental but theologically grounded: they understand enough to overlook offenses. Patience is the fruit of understanding.

V.17: the one who is kind to the poor (ḥônēn-dāl) is a lender to the LORD (malweh YHWH). The transaction is commercial in form but theological in content: kindness to the poor creates a debt that the LORD himself will repay. You are not giving to the poor; you are lending to God.
V.21: the LORD's purpose (ʿǎṣâ) is what prevails (tāqûm — stands, is established). The same sovereignty theme as 16:9. Human planning is real and multiplied; divine purpose is singular and final.

tx.title: 'What is desired in a person is kindness'
tx.content: MT: ta'avat adam chasdo (what is desired in a person is his hesed/loyalty). LXX reads quite differently. The MT places hesed as the supreme human desideratum.
tx.note: The MT's hesed-reading connects to Proverbs' broader theme: the supreme human virtue is covenant kindness. The verse continues: 'better to be poor than a liar' — placing poverty with hesed above wealth with deception.

debate.title: Proverbs 19:17 and the Theology of Poverty Care

positions.name: God identifies with the poor: a genuine theological claim
positions.argument: The loan-to-the-LORD metaphor makes an extraordinary claim: giving to the poor is a transaction with God. Matt 25:40 develops this: whatever you did for the least you did for Christ. The theology is genuine, not merely motivational.

positions.name: A practical motivational device, not strict theological identity
positions.argument: The commercial metaphor is primarily persuasive — using the reliability of God's repayment to encourage generosity. The theological claim about divine identity with the poor may read more into the metaphor than it intends.

scores.label: Integrity as More Valuable Than Wealth

themes.note: Proverbs 19 applies the wisdom value-hierarchy to economic and social life: integrity consistently outranks wealth; generosity to the poor is a covenant transaction with God; patience in overlooking offense is gloriously noble; the LORD's purpose prevails over all human planning.
